subject: emergency-medicine
title: Airway management
preambleMarkdown:
# Airway management



> **Clinical-review notice:** This is a bullet-first examination and clinical-orientation draft. Local protocols, medication doses, procedural thresholds, pregnancy/pediatric pathways, emergency workflows, and jurisdiction-specific guidance must be checked before clinical use.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Airway management is a clinical condition or syndrome in emergency medicine.
- This article focuses on triage, immediate threats, time-sensitive diagnostics, disposition, and safety-netting.
- The practical clinical priority is emergency medicine diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Start by deciding whether the presentation is stable, time-sensitive, complicated, recurrent, or incidental.
- Imported source notes are preserved below for clinical review and citation reconciliation.

sections.heading: Key Points
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify unstable, time-sensitive, or function-threatening presentations first.
- Anchor the assessment of Airway management to the patient context: age, pregnancy status when relevant, immune status, comorbid disease, medications, prior episodes, and local resources.
- Use focused history and examination before broad testing.
- Choose investigations that change diagnosis, severity assessment, treatment, disposition, or follow-up.
- Treat reversible danger early and give clear safety-net instructions.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Airway management is best defined by the specific clinical problem, anatomic site, mechanism, severity, and care setting.
- For this topic, the core concept is emergency medicine diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Separate suspected, confirmed, recurrent, complicated, and resolved disease states.

sections.heading: Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Acute, subacute, chronic, recurrent, or resolved state.
- Mild, moderate, severe, complicated, or life-threatening disease.
- Primary disease versus secondary complication or medication effect.
- Outpatient, emergency department, inpatient, ICU, pregnancy, pediatric, or older-adult setting.

sections.heading: Epidemiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The epidemiology of Airway management depends on age, sex, geography, exposure, comorbid disease, medication use, pregnancy status when relevant, and ascertainment setting.
- In emergency medicine, exam questions usually test the common population plus the high-risk exception.
- Risk estimates should be updated from local data or current guideline sources before publication.

sections.heading: Anatomy
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify the organ, tissue, vessel, nerve, compartment, joint, pathway, or system most directly involved in Airway management.
- Map nearby structures that explain referred pain, spread, obstruction, ischemia, neurologic deficits, imaging findings, or procedure risk.
- For non-anatomic topics, focus on the structure most likely to change symptoms, testing, or treatment.

sections.heading: Physiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Define the normal function that Airway management disrupts or depends on.
- Connect the physiology to symptoms, examination findings, laboratory abnormalities, imaging appearance, and treatment targets.
- Consider compensation, reserve, feedback loops, and decompensation triggers.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The central mechanism in Airway management should explain why the patient develops the key symptoms and complications.
- Mechanisms may include obstruction, inflammation, ischemia, infection, immune injury, degeneration, trauma, thrombosis, endocrine imbalance, toxicity, neoplasia, or functional dysregulation.
- Distinguish the primary process from secondary complications and treatment-related harm.

sections.heading: Etiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider the common causes of Airway management first, then actively check for dangerous but less common causes.
- Review congenital, acquired, infectious, inflammatory, traumatic, metabolic, neoplastic, medication-related, toxic, pregnancy-related, immune, and idiopathic causes when relevant.
- Use the time course and exposure history to narrow the cause.

sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Risk changes with age, family history, baseline disease, immune status, pregnancy, medications, procedures, travel, occupation, environment, and prior episodes.
- For Airway management, risk factors should guide pretest probability and prevention, not replace objective assessment.
- Document modifiable risks that can be addressed during treatment or follow-up.

sections.heading: Genetics
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider inherited disease when presentation is early, familial, recurrent, syndromic, unusually severe, or associated with congenital findings.
- Ask about family history, ancestry when clinically relevant, consanguinity, pregnancy losses, and similar symptoms in relatives.
- Use genetic testing when results change diagnosis, screening, treatment, reproductive counseling, or family risk assessment.

sections.heading: Microbiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider infection when fever, exposure, immunocompromise, procedure, device, wound, systemic inflammation, or rapid clinical decline is present.
- Identify the likely source, route of spread, organism group, need for cultures, need for isolation, and need for source control.
- Antimicrobial decisions should account for severity, host risk, allergies, renal or hepatic function, pregnancy status, local resistance, and prior cultures.

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Symptoms and signs depend on acuity, organ system involved, severity, comorbidities, medications, and prior episodes.
- Classic findings help recognition but absence of a classic finding does not exclude serious disease.
- Complications may be the first presentation in older adults, immunocompromised patients, and patients with limited follow-up.

sections.heading: History
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask onset, duration, progression, triggers, exposures, associated symptoms, prior episodes, and treatments already tried.
- Review medications, allergies, procedures, devices, travel, occupation, sexual history when relevant, pregnancy status when relevant, family history, and baseline function.
- For Airway management, ask targeted questions that separate uncomplicated disease from dangerous complications.

sections.heading: Physical Examination
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Start with vital signs, mental status, perfusion, respiratory effort, hydration, pain severity, and overall appearance.
- Perform a focused emergency medicine examination that directly tests the suspected mechanism.
- Look for systemic involvement, complications, mimics, and findings that change urgency or disposition.

sections.heading: Red Flags
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Shock, altered mental status, respiratory distress, severe hypoxemia, syncope, seizure, major bleeding, or rapidly progressive symptoms.
- Severe pain out of proportion, new neurologic deficit, threatened airway, sepsis, peritonitis, limb ischemia, compartment syndrome, or vision threat.
- Any Airway management presentation with organ dysfunction, pregnancy risk, immunocompromise, failed initial treatment, or diagnostic uncertainty needs a lower threshold for escalation.

sections.heading: Differential Diagnosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Compare Airway management with disorders that share the same dominant symptom, anatomic site, exposure, laboratory pattern, or imaging abnormality.
- Prioritize dangerous mimics that would be harmed by delay or by the wrong treatment.
- Use acuity, location, risk factors, examination findings, labs, imaging, and response to treatment to narrow the differential.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Approach
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- First decide whether Airway management is unstable, complicated, recurrent, incidental, or safe for outpatient workup.
- Use the highest-yield initial test for the suspected syndrome rather than ordering broad panels without a decision point.
- If early tests are negative but suspicion remains high, reassess the history, repeat focused examination, and choose second-line testing based on the remaining differential.

sections.heading: Investigations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Use labs to assess severity, complications, treatment safety, and alternative diagnoses.
- Use imaging, bedside tests, cultures, tissue sampling, functional testing, or procedures when results change management.
- Interpret results with pretest probability and clinical context.
- Avoid tests that add risk, delay, or incidental findings without changing the next step.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Criteria
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Diagnosis of Airway management should combine compatible clinical pattern with accepted laboratory, imaging, procedural, histologic, physiologic, or scoring criteria when available.
- Do not diagnose from one nonspecific abnormality when the full syndrome does not fit.
- Document uncertainty and the follow-up plan when the diagnosis remains provisional.

sections.heading: Severity Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Severity depends on stability, organ dysfunction, symptom burden, anatomic extent, complications, treatment risk, and ability to follow up.
- Identify who needs urgent referral, admission, ICU care, procedure, surgery, isolation, rehabilitation, or close outpatient review.
- Use validated disease-specific scoring or staging systems when they exist and are available locally.

sections.heading: Management
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Assess stability first, then treat the underlying cause and the complications already present.
- Use supportive care, medications, procedures, rehabilitation, and prevention according to severity and contraindications.
- Escalate to specialty care when diagnosis is uncertain, severe disease is present, or treatment risk is high.

### Initial Management

- Confirm whether Airway management is stable, unstable, incidental, recurrent, or complicated.
- Assess airway, breathing, circulation, mental status, pain, hydration, perfusion, and immediate threats.
- Escalate early when red flags, organ dysfunction, procedural need, or diagnostic uncertainty is present.

### Non-pharmacological Management

- Use education, observation, rehabilitation, isolation, procedural planning, or environmental change when it addresses emergency medicine diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Tailor activity, diet, wound, exposure, work, driving, return-to-play, or safety advice to the clinical setting.

### Pharmacological Management

- Use medication only when the indication is clear and contraindications have been checked.
- Verify dose, route, duration, interactions, allergies, renal and hepatic adjustment, pregnancy or lactation status, and monitoring.

### Procedures

- Use procedures when they confirm diagnosis, obtain tissue or fluid, provide source control, relieve obstruction, restore alignment, repair anatomy, or treat complications.
- Check consent, anatomy, imaging, bleeding risk, infection risk, sedation risk, equipment, and aftercare before proceeding.

### Surgical Management

- Surgical or procedural referral is needed for instability, failed medical management, obstruction, perforation, ischemia, uncontrolled bleeding, malignancy concern, repairable structural disease, or unsafe bedside management.
- Timing depends on severity, anatomy, operative risk, patient goals, and local expertise.

sections.heading: Monitoring
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Track symptoms, vital signs, function, pain, intake/output when relevant, focused examination findings, key labs, adverse effects, and response to therapy.
- For Airway management, monitoring should detect deterioration, treatment toxicity, recurrence, and missed complications.
- Escalate if expected improvement does not occur or new red flags appear.

sections.heading: Follow-up
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Confirm diagnosis, severity, treatment response, adherence, side effects, and patient understanding.
- Arrange repeat examination, labs, imaging, rehabilitation, surveillance, medication review, or specialty care when indicated.
- Give a clear reassessment interval and explicit return precautions.

sections.heading: Complications
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Complications of Airway management may be acute, chronic, recurrent, treatment-related, functional, psychosocial, or procedure-related.
- Prioritize complications that threaten life, airway, limb, vision, fertility, neurologic function, organ function, or public health.
- Document warning symptoms that require urgent reassessment.

sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Prognosis depends on cause, severity, timing of recognition, comorbid disease, age, treatment response, complications, and follow-up reliability.
- Early recognition of dangerous Airway management patterns improves disposition and reduces preventable complications.
- Published prognostic statistics should be checked against current specialty sources before release.

sections.heading: Prevention
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Address modifiable risk factors, medication safety, vaccination or prophylaxis when relevant, exposure reduction, screening, and recurrence prevention.
- For Airway management, prevention should target the cause, the complication pathway, and the follow-up failure points.
- Use patient-specific counseling rather than generic reassurance.

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Explain Airway management in plain language, including what is known, what remains uncertain, and what symptoms should trigger urgent care.
- Review medication purpose, expected benefit, side effects, duration, and what to do if symptoms worsen.
- Confirm the patient understands follow-up, restrictions, wound or device care when relevant, and return precautions.

sections.heading: Nursing Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Monitor vital signs, pain, function, mental status, intake/output when relevant, wounds or devices, and response to treatment.
- Escalate fever, bleeding, respiratory change, neurologic change, perfusion change, uncontrolled pain, medication reaction, or worsening function.
- Reinforce education, discharge instructions, safe mobility, adherence, and follow-up logistics.

sections.heading: Pharmacy Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Check allergies, renal and hepatic function, pregnancy or lactation status, interactions, duplication, contraindications, adherence barriers, and monitoring needs.
- For Airway management, medication review should include both treatment drugs and drugs that may cause, worsen, or mimic the problem.
- High-risk drugs require a documented indication, duration, monitoring plan, and counseling.

sections.heading: Dentistry Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask about bleeding risk, anticoagulants, antiplatelets, immunosuppression, prosthetic material, active infection, and relevant medication adverse effects before invasive dental work.
- Coordinate with the treating clinician when medication interruption, prophylaxis, or infection control is being considered.
- Airway management does not automatically imply dental antibiotic prophylaxis; use condition-specific indications and local guidance.

sections.heading: Special Populations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Pediatrics: use age-specific normal values, developmental context, weight-based dosing, caregiver education, and safeguarding awareness.
- Pregnancy: stabilize the mother first, choose pregnancy-aware testing and medications when possible, and involve obstetrics for significant disease.
- Older adults: consider frailty, atypical presentation, falls, delirium, kidney function, polypharmacy, cognition, and goals of care.
- Renal or hepatic impairment: adjust diagnostic contrast, medications, dosing, and monitoring.
- Immunocompromised patients: lower the threshold for atypical infection, severe disease, cultures, imaging, admission, and interaction review.

sections.heading: Guideline Comparison
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current guidance for Airway management may vary by country, specialty society, age group, pregnancy status, disease severity, and available resources.
- Compare local policy with major society guidance for diagnostic thresholds, treatment sequencing, drug choice, procedural timing, and follow-up intervals.
- Document where local practice differs from international guidance and why.

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stabilize first when the patient is unstable; diagnostic precision comes after immediate threats are controlled.
- Match each test to a decision it will change.
- Reassess after treatment because nonresponse is often a diagnostic clue.
- For Airway management, the highest-yield clinical move is to identify severity and the next safe action.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Treating Airway management as a label instead of confirming mechanism, severity, and complications.
- Ordering broad tests without a focused question.
- Missing red flags because the initial symptom sounds common.
- Ignoring pregnancy status, renal function, hepatic function, allergies, interactions, or follow-up barriers.
- Discharging without clear return precautions.

sections.heading: Exam Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Step 1: know the mechanism, anatomy, microbiology, pharmacology, or physiology behind the topic.
- Step 2 CK: know the next best diagnostic or management step.
- Step 3: know disposition, monitoring, complications, follow-up, and patient safety.
- Board exams: know contraindications, high-risk exceptions, and when to escalate.

sections.heading: High-Yield Facts
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Know the classic presentation, first diagnostic step, first treatment step, dangerous complication, and follow-up need.
- Severity and stability decide disposition.
- Treatment should include prevention of recurrence when possible.

sections.heading: Evidence Gaps
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- This Airway management draft still needs specialty review before publication.
- Medication doses, procedural thresholds, screening intervals, risk scores, and guideline claims should be localized.
- Imported notes, when present, should be reconciled with current evidence and cited sources.

sections.heading: References
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current specialty society guidance, local protocols, and institutional pathways should be checked during clinical review.
- When imported source notes are present, reconcile them against current references before publication.

sections.heading: Imported Source Notes

### Source Excerpt: Airway

_Source lines: 402-922._

```text
III. AIRWAY
A. Assessment: Airway management includes assessing immediate airway patency as
well as determining future risk of airway compromise.

1. Airway patency
a. The airway should be assessed for patency first by looking, listening, and
feeling for air exchange. The patient without spontaneous respirations
requires an attempt at ventilation to assess airway patency.

b. The most common cause of airway obstruction is prolapse of the tongue into the
posterior oropharynx. Physical examination may also reveal foreign body or fa-
cial, mandibular, or tracheal-laryngeal fractures resulting in airway obstruction.

2. Airway protection
a. Testing the gag reflex is one way to assess airway protection. This method pre-
dominantly assesses the sensory afferent component of cranial nerves IX and X.

b. A superior alternative is to assess the posterior oropharynx for pooled secre-
tions and, time permitting, to observe the patient for the ability to swallow.

Swallowing is the natural means of protecting the airway and clearing secre-
tions; a patient with pooled oral secretions requires definitive airway man-
agement. Intact swallowing requires coordinated function of the sensory
and motor components of cranial nerves V2 , V3 , IX, and X.

B. Interventions
1. General guidelines
a. Protection of the cervical spine. All trauma victims should be placed in a
protective cervical spine collar (this step is often performed in the prehos-
pital setting). However, intubation is best performed with the collar off and
experienced hands maintaining spinal immobilization.

b. Prevention of aspiration. Vomiting and aspiration are common in resuscita-
tion. Immediate suctioning of the lower pharynx and oropharynx is manda-
tory for proper airway management. A vomiting patient should be rolled to
the left lateral decubitus position and the entire spine properly protected so
the airway can be cleared.

2. Simple maneuvers and airway adjunct devices
a. Head tilt/chin lift. This maneuver is performed by simultaneously lifting
the chin forward while applying pressure to the forehead and is contraindi-
cated if neck trauma is suspected.

b. Jaw thrust. Applying pressure behind the angles of the mandible to thrust
the entire mandible forward simultaneously lifts the tongue and epiglottis
forward. The jaw thrust maneuver is the preferred method for patients with
possible cervical spine injury.

c. Oropharyngeal airway (OPA). Curved, hollow plastic device that is placed
over the top of the tongue. Its curved shape allows the distal portion of the
device to fit behind the base of the tongue, lifting it forward and preventing
obstruction.

i. Indications. Obstructed airway in an obtunded individual. Conscious
patients will not tolerate an OPA.

ii. Sizing. Compare its length to the distance between the corner of the
mouth and the angle of the mandible externally.

d. Nasopharyngeal airway (nasal trumpet). Soft rubber tube, 15 to 20 cm
long, which is lubricated and passed through an open nasal passage so the
distal tip lies behind the tongue.

i. Indications. Conscious and semiconscious patients can tolerate a naso-
pharyngeal airway. Use when oral trauma precludes OPA usage or when
an OPA may not be tolerated by a semiconscious or conscious patient
requiring limited airway management.

ii. Complications. Nasal trauma (sustained during placement) and
laryngospasm and vomiting in a conscious patient with a sensitive
oropharynx
e. Laryngeal mask airway (LMA). Composed of a mask with an inflatable
rim attached to a 15- to 20-cm long tube. The mask fits over the larynx the
Quick 	HIT

fall; listen and feel for air
exchange.

always assume cervical spi-
nal trauma.

same way a face mask fits over the nose and mouth. The tube communi-
cates with the mask allowing for direct ventilation of the larynx or trachea.

i. Indications. The LMA is very easy to place, even in the neutral position, and
is indicated when the airway cannot be secured by endotracheal intubation
in an unconscious patient. This device does not protect the airway from aspi-
ration and should be replaced as soon as possible with an endotracheal tube.

ii. Although the LMA is used frequently in the operating room, this
adjunct is not commonly used in the emergency department.

3. Intubation. A more secure airway than simple interventions. The treatment of
choice for any patient who is unable to safely maintain a patent airway or who
cannot sustain adequate ventilation
a. Orotracheal intubation. See Table 1-1.

b. Nasotracheal intubation. Easiest if the patient is awake and spontaneously
breathing. Use of a tube with directional tip control (e.g., a "ringed" or
Endotrol tube [Covidien, Dublin, Ireland]) may facilitate the procedure.

Guidelines for nasotracheal intubation are given in Table 1-2.

c. Rapid sequence intubation (RSI). A series of steps to maximize success and min-
imize complications. RSI incorporates muscle paralysis in association with seda-
tives. Sedative agents are not as effective as paralytic agents in decreasing muscle
tone, and they do not facilitate intubation to the degree provided by paralytics.

i. Overview
a) Indications and contraindications
1) Relative indications for RSI include the inability to cooperate with
intubation while awake, combative behavior, a depressed level of
consciousness, active seizure activity, clenched oral musculature,
severe trauma, and risk of complications resulting from intubation in
the setting of head injury, stroke, or aortic dissection (e.g., increased
intracranial pressure [ICP] or increased systolic blood pressure).

Quick 	HIT	HIT	T	T	T	T	T
Replace LMA as soon as
possible.

Quick 	HIT
Intubation is the treatment of
choice to maintain a patent
airway.

1. Determine tube size. Usual endotracheal tube sizes are 6.5-8.0 mm for women and 7.0-8.5 mm for men.

2. Assemble all equipment. Suction device, supplemental oxygen, bag-valve-mask device, endotracheal
tubes, cricothyroidotomy or needle-jet equipment, carbon dioxide indicator, laryngoscope, pulse oximeter
3. Prepare medications. Medications necessary for rapid sequence induction should also be assembled.a
4. Position the patient's head. "Sniffing" position: with the neck flexed on a pillow and the head extended.

5. Preoxygenate the patient. 100% oxygen for 5 minutes. Avoid unnecessary gastric filling by minimizing
assisted bag-valve-mask ventilation. The patient should be oxygenated to an oxygen saturation of 100%.

6. Position the laryngoscope. Handle in the left hand, inserting the laryngoscope along the right side
of the mouth to the base of the tongue and pushing the tongue to the left. If using a curved blade, ad-
vance the laryngoscope to the vallecula (superior to epiglottis) and lift anteriorly. If using a straight blade,
place the laryngoscope beneath the epiglottis and lift anteriorly.

7. Intubate the patient. Stop just after the cuff disappears behind the vocal cords. If the intubation
attempt is unsuccessful after 30 seconds or the oxygen saturation goes below 90%, stop and resume
bag-valve-mask ventilation before reattempting intubation.

8. Secure the tube. Use a syringe to inflate the cuff and attach the tube to bag valve or ventilator.

9. Confirm placement. Check for equal bilateral breath sounds and the absence of gastric air sounds
while bagging. Carbon dioxide monitoring or a chest radiograph may also be useful for checking tube
location. If any question remains regarding the placement of the endotracheal tube, repeat laryngoscopy
with the tube in place to be sure it is endotracheal.

10. Secure the tube. Use tape or tracheostomy ties and note the centimeter mark at the mouth. Suction the
patient's oropharynx and trachea.

aIf sedation or paralysis is employed, the patient is at risk for vomiting and aspiration.

Quick 	HIT
In children, the diameter
of the tube is calculated by
adding 16 to the patient's
age and dividing by 4.

Quick 	HIT
Maintain cervical immobili-
zation when spinal instability
is of concern (e.g., in trauma
victims).

2) Relative contraindications include airway distortion (which
could interfere with the ability to intubate once the patient is
paralyzed) and the presence of viable alternatives to RSI.

b) Goals. The goals of RSI are to:
1) Preoxygenate the patient
2) Avoid positive-pressure ventilation
3) Induce unawareness
4) Prevent complications, including aspiration
5) Atraumatically intubate the patient
c) Guidelines for performing RSI are summarized in Table 1-3.

ii. Pharmacologic adjuncts
a) Atropine (0.01 mg/kg). Prevents muscarinic bradycardia, which can
be associated with the administration of succinylcholine (especially
in children). Atropine should be strongly considered for children
younger than 6 years who require RSI.

b) Lidocaine (1.5 mg/kg administered intravenously approximately
3 minutes before intubation). May blunt the increases in ICP, systolic
blood pressure, and pulse that are usually associated with intubation,
and it may have other direct benefits in patients with injured brain
tissue. Although the efficacy of lidocaine administration is controver-
sial, the administration of lidocaine may be helpful and is unlikely
to be harmful, and lidocaine is readily available and inexpensive.

Fentanyl appears to have some role in these patients as well, with
the effect of blunting the catecholamine surge during laryngoscopy.

iii. Induction of unawareness. Sedative-induction agents are used to
decrease consciousness during intubation. Properties of these agents are
summarized in Tables 1-4 and 1-5.

a) Thiopental
1) Effects. Lowers the ICP by reducing cerebral blood flow. Rapid
redistribution of thiopental out of the brain results in a short
duration of action and light anesthesia. Thiopental, a myocardial
depressant, decreases systolic and mean arterial pressure.

1. Determine tube size. Common tube sizes are 6-7 mm for women and 7-8 mm for men.

2. Administer medications.

a. Spray the nasal passage with a vasoconstrictor spray such as cocaine 4% (4 mL) or phenylephrine
0.25% (2 mL), unless contraindicated.

b. Apply a topical anesthetic (e.g., lidocaine gel or a topical spray).

c. If sedation is required, administer fentanyl (1 !g/kg) or midazolam (0.05 to 0.1 mg/kg) and titrate to
effect.

3. Position the patient. Nasotracheal intubation may be performed with the patient sitting up.

4. Intubate the patient. Place the tube in the nasal passage and guide it into the nasopharynx. Monitor
progress by listening for air movement and observing fogging of the tube. As the tube enters the
oropharynx, gradually guide the tube downward. If using a tube with a directional tip control, pull on the
ring to direct the tube anteriorly. If the sounds stop, withdraw the tube approximately 1 to 2 cm until
breath sounds can be heard again. Reposition the tube, extending the patient's head if necessary. Suc-
cessful intubation occurs when the tube passes through the cords; the patient may cough and breath
sounds will reach maximum intensity if the tube is correctly positioned.

5. Confirm the placement of the tube. Check for equal bilateral breath sounds and the absence of gastric
air sounds while bagging. Carbon dioxide monitoring, a syringe test, or a chest radiograph may also be
useful for checking tube location. If any question remains regarding the placement of the endotracheal
tube, repeat laryngoscopy with the tube in place to be sure it is endotracheal.

Guidelines for Nasotracheal Intubation	TABLE 1-2
R E S U S C I T A T I O N 	 	5

1. Decide whether to use a depolarizing or nondepolarizing agent.

2. Decide whether a priming dose of a nondepolarizing agent is indicated.

3. Assemble and check all equipment, including a pulse oximeter, a cricothyroidotomy tray, a Yankauer
suction, laryngoscope blades, and a carbon dioxide detector. Include equipment necessary for an
alternative plan if the intubation attempt is unsuccessful (e.g., cricothyroidotomy, bag-valve-mask
ventilation, transtracheal needle-jet insufflation).

4. Reassure the patient and describe the procedure.

5. Preoxygenate the patient with 100% oxygen. Avoid gastric filling by avoiding unnecessary respiratory assistance.

6. Premedicate the patient to block increases in intracranial pressure and blood pressure as indicated by
the patient's condition. Consider lidocaine for patients in whom increased intracranial pressure and blood
pressure are a concern, and fentanyl for patients in whom increased blood pressure is a concern.

7. Administer a priming dose of the nondepolarizing agent if the decision is made to prime.

8. Sedate the patient using the agent of choice.

9. Paralyze the patient using the agent of choice.

10. Intubate the patient.

11. Confirm placement of the tube by checking for equal bilateral breath sounds and the absence of gastric
air sounds while bagging. Carbon dioxide monitoring or a chest radiograph may be useful for checking
tube location. If any question remains regarding placement of the endotracheal tube, repeat laryngoscopy
with the tube in place to be sure it is endotracheal.

12. Continue sedation, analgesia, and paralysis as appropriate for the patient's condition.

2) Adverse effects. Airway hyperactivity and laryngospasm follow-
ing mechanical stimulation of the airway during instrumentation.

Hypotension may also occur.

3) Contraindications. Hemodynamic instability
b) Methohexital. Lowers the ICP and has the same adverse effects as
thiopental
c) Fentanyl. Rarely used as a sole inducing agent
1) Effects. Fentanyl appears to blunt increases in blood pressure
and pulse associated with intubation and thus may be useful for
patients in whom such increases could be catastrophic.

2) Adverse effects. Fentanyl can cause bradycardia and hypoten-
sion secondary to parasympathetic stimulation and blunting of
catecholinergic activity.

d) Midazolam. Minimal cardiovascular effects and may blunt the in-
crease in ICP associated with intubation. Additionally, it is a drug
familiar to most emergency physicians.

e) Etomidate. Minimal cardiovascular effects (as compared with
thiopental). Often used for induction of trauma patients. It has a
rapid onset and short duration of action.

1) Adverse effects. Etomidate can suppress cortisol production,
even with a single dose.

2) Contraindications. Etomidate is contraindicated in children
younger than 10 years and in pregnant or lactating women.

f) Propofol. Lowers ICP and causes as much or greater hypotension
when compared with thiopental. It has a rapid onset of action as well
as rapid resolution of sedation.

g) Ketamine
1) Effects. Chemically related to phencyclidine and causes a dis-
sociative, cataleptic state. Ketamine also has analgesic properties.

Ketamine relaxes bronchial smooth muscle and rarely causes
respiratory depression. These characteristics make ketamine a
particularly appropriate induction agent for patients with isolated
respiratory failure, especially asthmatics.

2) Adverse effects. Dreams or hallucinations occur in 30% to 50%
of adults and 5% to 10% of prepubescent children. These may
be recalled as being dysphoric. Benzodiazepine medications are
given to minimize this side effect. Ketamine increases skeletal
muscle tone and is associated with nonpurposeful movements.

R E S U S C I T A T I O N 	 	7

Diseases of the Cardiovascular System
Resuscitation
It increases the mean arterial pressure, the pulse rate, and possi-
bly the ICP.

3) Contraindications. Patients with elevated ICP and those in whom
an increase in mean arterial pressure could be harmful.

iv. Neuromuscular blockade. To relax musculature to facilitate intubation.

Neuromuscular transmission is mediated by acetylcholine (ACh), which
is created from acetyl coenzyme A and choline. ACh is stored in synap-
tic vesicles in axon terminals and, after it is released, is hydrolyzed by
acetylcholinesterase in the synaptic cleft or by pseudocholinesterase in
the plasma.

a) Depolarizing blockade. Achieved with succinylcholine which
binds to end-plate ACh sites and causes depolarization in the same
manner as ACh. Succinylcholine results in a brief period of repeti-
tive muscle contractions manifested by fasciculations, followed by
block of neuromuscular transmission.

1) Dose. Intravenously (1.5 mg/kg for adults and children older than
10 to 12 years); 100 mg is the average adult dose. Dosing is in-
creased to 2 mg/kg for children younger than 10 to 12 years of age.

2) Onset and duration of action. Succinylcholine is the fastest act-
ing paralytic agent-satisfactory relaxation occurs within 30 to
60 seconds. Succinylcholine is hydrolyzed rapidly by pseudocho-
linesterase and as a result has the shortest duration of action of the
paralytic agents (3 to 5 minutes, occasionally as long as 10 minutes).

3) Adverse effects. Increased intragastric and intraocular pressures.

It is unclear whether succinylcholine alone causes increased ICP.

Succinylcholine has also been associated with malignant hyper-
thermia and arrhythmias (especially bradycardia in children).

Succinylcholine is associated with risk for prolonged paralysis
in patients with abnormal pseudocholinesterase levels (e.g.,
some pregnant women, patients with severe hepatic dysfunction,
patients with renal failure, and patients with bronchogenic car-
cinoma). Due to release of potassium from muscle tissue during
depolarization, succinylcholine can lead to lethal arrhythmias.

4) Contraindications. Penetrating ocular trauma or glaucoma,
patients with unstable fractures (which may be worsened by
fasciculations), patients with neuromuscular diseases, and burn
patients more than 24 hours after injury
b) Nondepolarizing blockade. Competitive inhibition of postsynaptic
ACh receptors. Nondepolarizing agents cause more prolonged
paralysis than succinylcholine (Table 1-6).

1) Vecuronium. Lacks any histamine-releasing or ganglion-
blocking activity. Advantages include no fasciculations and no
increase in ICP, intragastric pressure, or intraocular pressure.

Vecuronium has a long duration of action; therefore, it must be
used with caution because if the attempt at intubation fails, the
patient will require prolonged ventilatory support using bag-
valve-mask ventilation until another attempt can be made.

2) Pancuronium. May increase the pulse and blood pressure,
although the increase is not usually clinically significant.

3) Rocuronium. Newer nondepolarizing agent-created in an
attempt to achieve the rapid onset and short duration of action
of succinylcholine. Rocuronium has the fastest onset of the
nondepolarizing agents (see Table 1-6).

c) "Priming." Administration of a less-than-paralytic dose of a nonde-
polarizing agent (e.g., vecuronium, 0.01 mg/kg) to bind postsynap-
tic ACh receptor sites prior to the administration of a second dose
of a depolarizing paralytic agent. Evidence is controversial. Priming
blocks succinylcholine-induced fasciculations and their sequelae,
```
